summary: llm-context-compressor ships system-intel exfiltration trap module
details: The package contains lib/trap-core.js, a ~1000-line module that combines system-information collection (os.hostname(), os.platform()), filesystem enumeration (fs.existsSync), shell command execution (multiple require('child_process') call sites at lines 12, 748, 951, 959, 964, including invocation of 'curl' at line 781 and 'ping' at line 40), and outbound HTTPS POST requests (require('https') at line 8, with POST request bodies at lines 385, 411, 466, 548, 549 carrying a 'hostname:' field at lines 393, 411, 553, 600, 1023). The structural shape — collect host identifiers and system info, then POST them to remote endpoints, with child_process available for command execution — is the canonical system-intel exfiltration / RAT pattern. The file name 'trap-core' itself is consistent with the author's intent to ship a trap. A package marketed as an 'LLM context compressor' has no legitimate need for a module that gathers hostname/platform data and POSTs it off-host alongside curl/ping invocation and shell execution capability.

cwes.description: The product contains code that appears to be malicious in nature.
cwes.name: Embedded Malicious Code
